hazard.summary: The client appears to ignore or fail to honor long Retry-After windows on 429 responses, while its own HTTP timeout converts server-directed waiting into immediate retry behavior. This creates rapid retry loops, quota depletion, and false liveness under sustained rate limiting.
signals: 429 responses retried immediately without honoring Retry-After
20+ retries per minute during rate limiting
GitHub requests wait times of 5-10 minutes via Retry-After
hold durations over 60 seconds cause timeout and immediate retry
rate limit penalties escalate from 5min to 10min to 15min+

constraints: Retry-After must be treated as a hard lower bound, not a hint.
Transport-layer timeouts must not override application-layer waiting intent from a 429 response.
Retryable paths must have an explicit retry budget or max elapsed boundary.
Long Retry-After windows must be surfaced as non-retryable within the current execution context when recovery cannot happen inside the run.
verification: Simulate a 429 response with Retry-After greater than 5 minutes and verify the client does not immediately retry inside the same run.
Verify transport timeouts longer than 30-60 seconds do not reclassify server-directed waiting as connection failure.
Verify retries stop after an explicit retry budget or max elapsed boundary.
Verify user-facing state surfaces cooldown or quota exhaustion instead of looping silently.
notes: This receipt adds a strong cross-layer pattern to the corpus: application-layer wait signals can be nullified by transport-layer timeout behavior, turning explicit server guidance into uncontrolled retry loops. The important lesson is not merely 'add backoff' but 'preserve server intent across layers and distinguish WAIT from STOP.'

constraints: surface long cooldown windows instead of looping
anti_patterns: transport timeout reclassifies wait as failure
